title: 1 Chronicles 13
subtitle: First Attempt to Move the Ark — Uzzah Dies

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ark Brought to Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — David Consults All Israel; the Procession Begins

refs.note: The parallel in Samuel focuses on David and 30,000 chosen men. The Chronicler expands the participants to “all Israel.”
The ark at Kiriath Jearim for 20 years. The Chronicler doesn’t narrate this background but assumes it.

notes.note: David’s attention to the ark teaches leaders to prioritise God’s presence in their communities. The first act of a righteous king is not military reform but worship reform. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: “We did not inquire of it” — the verb darash (“seek/inquire”) is the Chronicler’s key theological term. Not seeking the ark = not seeking God. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David consults “the whole assembly of Israel” about bringing the ark from Kiriath Jearim, where it has rested since the Philistines returned it (1 Sam 7:1). The Chronicler adds a detail absent from Samuel: “Let us also send word to the rest of our people throughout the territories of Israel... to come and join us.” The “all Israel” theme extends to the ark’s transport — this is a national event, not a royal decision. The ark is placed on a new cart and the procession begins with music and celebration.

heb.paragraph: Dāraš is the Chronicler's signature verb — appearing more than 40 times in 1-2 Chronicles, far more frequently than in Kings. 'To seek the LORD' is the defining act of faithful kingship in the Chronicler's theology. Kings who seek (dāraš) prosper; kings who forsake (ʿāzaḇ) are abandoned. This binary drives the entire narrative.

notes.note: The Chronicler's portrait of David emphasizes his role as the ideal theocratic king — the man after God's own heart who established worship, organized the Levites, and prepared for the temple. David's reign points typologically to Christ, the Son of David, who perfectly fulfills every dimension of the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16; Rom 1:3).

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 9–14 — Uzzah Touches the Ark and Dies; the Ark Diverted

refs.note: “The Kohathites are to carry the holy things... they must not touch the holy things or they will die.” The law Uzzah violated.
David’s later explanation: “The LORD our God broke out against us because we did not inquire of him about how to do it in the prescribed way.”

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase “because he had put his hand on the ark” (shalach yadō) uses the same language as “stretching out the hand” against something sacred. The act, however reflexive, crossed a covenantal boundary. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: At the threshing floor of Kidon, the oxen stumble. Uzzah reaches out to steady the ark and is struck dead. David is angry and afraid: he names the place Perez Uzzah (“outbreak against Uzzah”) and abandons the transport. The ark goes to the house of Obed-Edom the Gittite, where it remains three months, blessing his household. The episode teaches a brutal lesson: holy objects require holy handling. The cart was an innovation; the law required Levites to carry the ark on poles (Exod 25:14; Num 4:15). David’s zeal was genuine but his method was wrong.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe then said to the whole assembly of Israel, \u201cIf it seems good to you and if it is the will of the Lord our God, let us send word far and wide to the rest of our people throughout the territories of Israel, and also to the priests and Levites who are with them in their towns and pasturelands, to come and join us.ESVAnd David said to all the assembly of Israel, \u201cIf it seems good to you and from the LORD our God, let us send abroad to our brothers who remain in all the lands of Israel, as well as to the priests and Levites in the cities that have pasturelands, that they may be gathered to us.NIVDavid and all Israel went to Baalah of Judah (Kiriath Jearim) to bring up from there the ark of God the Lord, who is enthroned between the cherubim\u2014the ark that is called by the Name.ESVAnd David and all Israel went up to Baalah, that is, to Kiriath-jearim that belongs to Judah, to bring up from there the ark of God, which is called by the name of the LORD who sits enthroned above the cherubim.

themes.note: First Attempt to Move the Ark — Uzzah Dies

coaching.tip: Uzzah's death when the ark is transported on a cart — rather than carried by Levites as prescribed — is kept in Chronicles despite the Chronicler's positive portrait of David. The lesson is too important to omit: good intentions with wrong methods do not equal obedience.

prayer_prompt: Uzzah died when the ark was transported incorrectly — good intentions, wrong method. Consider asking God about the places where your zeal for Him has ignored His instructions.
